question_text: Which of the following gases released primarily by the burning of fossil fuels in vehicles and industries is a major contributor to the greenhouse effect and global warming?

options.text: Oxygen (O₂), which is essential for human respiration and sustaining life
Carbon dioxide (CO₂), which traps heat in the atmosphere and contributes to rising global temperatures
Nitrogen (N₂), which makes up approximately seventy-eight percent of the Earth's atmosphere
Helium (He), which is an inert noble gas used in balloons and scientific instruments

explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is the primary greenhouse gas released by burning fossil fuels (petrol, diesel, coal, natural gas) in vehicles, factories and power plants. CO₂ traps heat in the atmosphere (greenhouse effect), contributing to global warming and climate change — a major environmental health concern.
elimination_hints: O₂ (A) supports life; N₂ (C) is inert and abundant; He (D) is a noble gas. Only CO₂ (B) is a greenhouse gas linked to fossil fuel combustion.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes the concept of 'environmental hygiene' and its relationship with community health in preventing the spread of diseases?

options.text: Environmental hygiene means cleaning only the interior of individual homes and has no connection to community health or disease prevention

Environmental hygiene involves maintaining clean and safe physical surroundings — proper waste disposal, clean water supply, effective drainage, vector control and sanitation — which collectively reduce the risk of communicable disease transmission in the community
Environmental hygiene is relevant only in hospitals and has no application in residential areas or public spaces
Environmental hygiene refers exclusively to planting decorative flowers in public parks for aesthetic beauty

explanation: Environmental hygiene encompasses all measures to maintain clean, safe physical surroundings: proper solid waste disposal, clean drinking water supply, effective drainage and sewage systems, vector control (eliminating mosquito breeding sites), sanitation facilities and clean public spaces. These collectively reduce disease transmission at the community level.
elimination_hints: A limits to individual homes; C limits to hospitals; D focuses only on aesthetics. B correctly describes the comprehensive community-wide scope of environmental hygiene.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze why athletes training outdoors in cities with high levels of air pollution face greater health risks than sedentary individuals living in the same polluted environment.

options.text: Athletes face the same risk as sedentary individuals because air pollution affects everyone equally regardless of activity level

Athletes breathe significantly more air per minute during intense exercise due to higher ventilation rates, inhaling a substantially larger volume of pollutants deep into the lungs, and their airways are more dilated during exercise allowing pollutants to penetrate deeper into the respiratory tract
Athletes are completely immune to air pollution because regular exercise strengthens the lungs against all harmful substances
Sedentary individuals actually face greater risk because their lungs are weaker and less able to filter pollutants

explanation: During intense exercise, ventilation rate increases dramatically (from ~6 L/min at rest to 100+ L/min during vigorous exercise). Athletes therefore inhale a far greater volume of polluted air per session. Additionally, exercise causes a shift from nasal to mouth breathing, bypassing nasal filtration, and bronchial dilation allows pollutants to penetrate deeper into the respiratory tract.
elimination_hints: A ignores the ventilation rate difference; C wrongly claims immunity; D reverses the dose-response logic. B correctly explains the higher pollutant dose from increased ventilation.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate the effectiveness of 'green belts' (bands of trees and vegetation planted along highways and around industrial areas) as a measure for reducing air and noise pollution in urban areas.

options.text: Green belts have no effect on pollution levels and are planted only for decorative purposes

Green belts are effective because trees absorb CO₂ and other gaseous pollutants, trap particulate matter on leaf surfaces, release oxygen, act as sound barriers that attenuate noise levels, reduce wind-carried dust, and provide psychological well-being benefits — making them a multi-functional pollution mitigation strategy
Green belts increase air pollution because trees produce carbon dioxide at night through respiration
Green belts are effective only against water pollution and have no impact on air or noise pollution

explanation: Green belts serve multiple pollution-mitigation functions: trees absorb CO₂ and gaseous pollutants (SO₂, NO₂), leaf surfaces trap PM and dust, trees release O₂ through photosynthesis, dense vegetation acts as a natural sound barrier attenuating noise, reduces wind-carried dust, and provides aesthetic and psychological well-being benefits. This makes green belts a cost-effective, multi-functional mitigation strategy.
elimination_hints: A denies documented benefits; C exaggerates nighttime respiration (net effect is strongly positive); D wrongly limits to water pollution. B comprehensively lists the multi-functional benefits.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly identifies a specific occupational health hazard faced by professional athletes and sports coaches who train and compete in extreme outdoor heat conditions during Indian summers?

options.text: Frostbite from exposure to extremely cold temperatures during summer training

Heat exhaustion, heat stroke and severe dehydration resulting from prolonged exertion in high ambient temperature and humidity conditions, which can be life-threatening if not recognised and treated promptly
Altitude sickness from training at high elevations in desert regions of Rajasthan
Seasickness from training on outdoor sports fields during the monsoon season

explanation: Professional athletes and coaches training in Indian summer conditions (temperatures often exceeding 40°C with high humidity) face serious occupational heat-related hazards: heat exhaustion (profuse sweating, weakness, nausea), heat stroke (body temperature >40°C, confusion, organ failure) and severe dehydration. These are occupational hazards specific to outdoor sports professions in tropical climates.
elimination_hints: A describes cold injury (not relevant in summer); C misapplies altitude sickness to deserts; D confuses seasickness with outdoor field training. B correctly identifies heat-related occupational hazards.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following is a major source of water pollution in India that introduces pathogenic microorganisms into rivers and lakes, causing waterborne diseases in downstream communities?

options.text: Release of purified mineral water from bottling plants into rivers
Discharge of untreated or partially treated sewage and domestic wastewater directly into rivers and lakes
Collection of rainwater in rooftop tanks for household use
Installation of water filtration systems in urban water treatment facilities

explanation: Untreated or partially treated sewage discharge is the largest source of pathogenic water pollution in India. Human waste contains disease-causing bacteria, viruses and parasites that contaminate rivers and lakes. Downstream communities using this water for drinking, bathing and cooking are at high risk of waterborne diseases like cholera, typhoid and dysentery.
elimination_hints: A (purified water) and D (filtration systems) actually improve water quality; C is a safe water harvesting practice. Only B introduces pathogens into water bodies.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze the concept of 'indoor air pollution' and explain why it is considered a significant environmental health concern in rural Indian households that use traditional biomass fuels for cooking.

options.text: Indoor air pollution is not a concern in rural India because rural homes always have excellent ventilation systems

Burning biomass fuels like wood, cow dung and crop residues in poorly ventilated traditional kitchens releases high concentrations of PM2.5, carbon monoxide, formaldehyde and other toxic gases, and women and children who spend the most time near the cooking area suffer chronic respiratory diseases, eye infections and are at increased risk of lung cancer
Indoor air pollution only occurs in urban air-conditioned offices and never in rural homes
Biomass fuels produce cleaner air than LPG gas and are recommended by health authorities for all households

explanation: Indoor air pollution from biomass cooking is a major rural health crisis: burning wood, cow dung and crop residues in enclosed, poorly ventilated kitchens creates PM2.5 concentrations many times above safe limits, along with CO, formaldehyde and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Women and children disproportionately affected — chronic bronchitis, COPD, eye infections, and increased lung cancer risk.
elimination_hints: A ignores poor rural ventilation reality; C wrongly limits to urban offices; D contradicts evidence (biomass is far dirtier than LPG). B accurately describes the rural indoor air pollution crisis.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following prevention strategies is most effective for reducing noise-induced hearing loss among workers in a loud industrial factory environment where machinery operates above 90 decibels continuously?

options.text: Asking workers to sing loudly to drown out the machine noise during their shift

Providing workers with properly fitted ear protection devices (earplugs or earmuffs), limiting exposure time through shift rotation, installing sound-dampening enclosures around loud machinery, and conducting regular hearing tests for early detection of damage
Painting the factory walls in bright colours to distract workers from noticing the noise
Increasing the factory temperature to make workers sweat, which is believed to protect the ears

explanation: Comprehensive noise protection in factories includes: personal protective equipment (earplugs rated 25-30 dB reduction, earmuffs), administrative controls (shift rotation to limit exposure duration), engineering controls (sound-dampening enclosures, vibration isolation), and health surveillance (regular audiometric testing for early detection). This multi-layered approach is the standard occupational health practice.
elimination_hints: A would damage hearing further; C has no noise-reducing effect; D is physiologically nonsensical. B correctly describes the standard multi-layered occupational noise protection approach.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate the impact of the Indian government's Swachh Bharat Mission (Clean India Mission) on environmental hygiene and community health, particularly in terms of sanitation and waterborne disease prevention.

options.text: The Swachh Bharat Mission focused only on building luxury housing and had no sanitation or hygiene component

The mission has significantly improved sanitation by constructing millions of household and community toilets, reducing open defecation, promoting waste management, and raising public awareness about hygiene — which collectively help reduce faecal-oral disease transmission and improve community health outcomes
The mission worsened environmental hygiene by encouraging the dumping of waste in public spaces
The Swachh Bharat Mission was launched by the United Nations and had no involvement of the Indian government

explanation: The Swachh Bharat Mission (launched 2014) has significantly impacted sanitation: construction of over 100 million household toilets, reduction in open defecation, solid waste management initiatives, and massive public awareness campaigns about hygiene. These interventions reduce faecal contamination of water and soil, breaking the faecal-oral transmission chain for waterborne diseases.
elimination_hints: A misidentifies the mission's focus; C claims the opposite effect; D wrongly attributes it to the UN (it is a Government of India initiative). B accurately describes the mission's sanitation achievements and health impact.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze how agricultural activities such as excessive use of chemical fertilisers and pesticides contribute to both water pollution and soil pollution, creating a dual environmental health threat.

options.text: Agricultural chemicals have no environmental impact because they are completely absorbed by the crops and leave no residue

Excess fertilisers and pesticides that are not absorbed by crops wash into rivers and groundwater during irrigation and rainfall (causing water pollution and eutrophication), while simultaneously accumulating in the soil (causing soil pollution), contaminating the food chain and posing health risks including cancer, endocrine disruption and neurological damage
Agricultural chemicals improve water quality by adding nutrients that purify rivers and lakes naturally
Pesticides affect only the insects they target and have absolutely no impact on soil, water or human health

explanation: Excess agricultural chemicals create dual pollution: fertiliser runoff causes water eutrophication (algal blooms, oxygen depletion, dead zones), while pesticide runoff contaminates water supplies. Simultaneously, these chemicals accumulate in soil, are absorbed by food crops, and enter the food chain — causing bioaccumulation and health effects including cancer, endocrine disruption and neurological damage in humans.
elimination_hints: A claims complete absorption (untrue); C wrongly says chemicals purify water; D denies well-documented non-target effects. B correctly traces the dual water-soil contamination pathway.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following air pollutants in Column A with their primary health effect on the human body in Column B.

column_b.text: Penetrates deep into lungs and bloodstream, causes cardiovascular disease and lung cancer
Binds to haemoglobin reducing oxygen-carrying capacity of blood, causes headaches and at high levels can be fatal
Causes neurological damage, developmental delays in children and kidney dysfunction
Irritates airways and triggers asthma attacks, contributes to acid rain formation

explanation: CO → binds haemoglobin, reduces oxygen capacity (Q). SO₂ → airway irritation, asthma, acid rain (S). PM2.5 → deep lung/bloodstream penetration, CVD, lung cancer (P). Lead → neurological damage, child developmental delays, kidney damage (R).
elimination_hints: CO (1) is the classic 'silent killer' that binds haemoglobin (Q); lead (4) is known for neurological damage in children (R). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following environmental health challenges in Column A with the most appropriate government programme or international initiative designed to address each challenge in Column B.

column_a.text: Open defecation and poor sanitation in rural India
Reducing vehicular air pollution in Indian cities
Global climate change from greenhouse gas emissions
Indoor air pollution from biomass cooking in rural households

column_b.text: Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana — providing free LPG connections to BPL households to replace biomass cooking
Paris Climate Agreement — international treaty to limit global temperature rise by reducing emissions
Swachh Bharat Mission — building toilets and promoting sanitation and hygiene behaviour change
Bharat Stage (BS-VI) emission norms — stricter vehicle emission standards for cleaner fuel combustion

explanation: Open defecation → Swachh Bharat Mission (R). Vehicular pollution → BS-VI emission norms (S). Climate change → Paris Agreement (Q). Indoor biomass pollution → Ujjwala Yojana free LPG (P).
elimination_hints: Swachh Bharat (R) is the toilet-building programme — matches sanitation (1). Ujjwala Yojana (P) is the LPG programme — matches biomass cooking (4). Anchor with these two.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following noise levels in Column A with the source or environment that typically produces that level in Column B.

column_b.text: Rock concert or nightclub at close range — pain threshold, immediate hearing damage risk
Quiet library or bedroom at night — comfortable, no health risk
Busy street traffic or vacuum cleaner — moderate, can cause stress with prolonged exposure
Power tools, chainsaw or loud music in headphones — high risk of hearing damage with sustained exposure

explanation: 30-40 dB → quiet library/bedroom (Q). 70-80 dB → busy traffic/vacuum cleaner (R). 100-110 dB → power tools/chainsaw (S). 120-130 dB → rock concert, pain threshold (P). The scale progresses from whisper-quiet to pain threshold.
elimination_hints: 30-40 dB (1) is very quiet = library (Q); 120-130 dB (4) is extremely loud = pain threshold (P). Anchor with these extremes.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the relationship between deforestation and air pollution.
assertion: Large-scale deforestation contributes to increased air pollution and elevated carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere.
reason: Trees absorb carbon dioxide during photosynthesis and release oxygen. When forests are destroyed, this carbon sink is lost, and the burning of cleared forest biomass releases additional CO₂ into the atmosphere.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — deforestation increases air pollution and CO₂. The Reason correctly explains the dual mechanism: loss of the carbon sink (trees no longer absorb CO₂) plus release of stored carbon from burning cleared biomass. Both mechanisms directly increase atmospheric CO₂.
elimination_hints: Both statements are well-established environmental science facts. The Reason explains the precise dual mechanism (lost sink + biomass burning) that causes the Assertion's increased CO₂.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding occupational health hazards and the sports industry.
assertion: Sports coaches, athletes and PE teachers face unique occupational health hazards that differ from those in traditional office or factory environments.
reason: Sports professionals are exposed to risks such as chronic musculoskeletal injuries from repetitive training, heat-related illnesses from outdoor exposure, skin damage from prolonged UV radiation, vocal strain from shouting instructions, and psychological stress from competitive pressure — hazards specific to the nature of their work.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — sports professionals face unique occupational hazards. The Reason is also true and provides specific examples: repetitive musculoskeletal injuries, heat illness, UV skin damage, vocal strain and competitive psychological stress. These are directly linked to the nature of sports work, explaining why these hazards differ from office/factory environments.
elimination_hints: Both statements are factually accurate. The Reason provides specific sport-related hazard examples that explain the Assertion's claim of unique occupational risks.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding noise pollution and its regulation in India.
assertion: The use of loudspeakers and public address systems beyond permissible noise limits is prohibited in residential areas of India under the Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000.
reason: Excessive loudspeaker use in residential areas disturbs sleep, causes hearing damage, triggers stress responses, affects concentration and reduces the overall quality of life for community members.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — India has Noise Pollution Rules (2000) that regulate loudspeaker use in residential areas. The Reason is also true and explains why such regulation is necessary: the health effects (sleep disturbance, hearing damage, stress, reduced concentration, quality of life) provide the public health justification for the legal restriction.
elimination_hints: Both statements are factually correct. The Reason provides the health justification that explains why the regulatory prohibition in the Assertion exists.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following water treatment steps in the correct order as typically followed in a municipal water treatment plant to convert raw river water into safe drinking water for the community.
items: I. Disinfection — adding chlorine or ozone to kill remaining bacteria, viruses and parasites
II. Screening and sedimentation — removing large debris and allowing heavy particles to settle
III. Filtration — passing water through sand and gravel filters to remove remaining fine particles
IV. Distribution — pumping treated water through pipelines to households and public taps

options.text: II → III → I → IV
I → IV → II → III
III → I → IV → II
IV → II → III → I

explanation: Water treatment sequence: Screening/sedimentation (II, removes large particles) → Filtration (III, removes fine particles through sand/gravel) → Disinfection (I, kills pathogens with chlorine/ozone) → Distribution (IV, delivers safe water to consumers). This follows the standard coarse-to-fine-to-disinfect-to-distribute sequence.
elimination_hints: Screening large debris (II) must come first; distribution to households (IV) must come last. Anchor with these endpoints.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following environmental sounds in order of increasing noise level from the quietest to the loudest.
items: I. Rock concert near the speakers (approximately 120 dB)
II. Normal conversation between two people (approximately 60 dB)
III. Whisper in a quiet room (approximately 20 dB)
IV. Busy city traffic intersection during rush hour (approximately 85 dB)

options.text: III → II → IV → I
I → IV → II → III
II → III → I → IV
IV → I → III → II

explanation: Noise level order (quietest to loudest): Whisper ~20 dB (III) → Normal conversation ~60 dB (II) → Busy traffic ~85 dB (IV) → Rock concert ~120 dB (I). This demonstrates the logarithmic decibel scale from whisper-quiet to pain threshold.
elimination_hints: Whisper (III) is the quietest; rock concert (I) is the loudest. These anchor both endpoints clearly.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: True or False: Air pollution is caused by the introduction of harmful substances such as particulate matter, carbon monoxide, sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides into the atmosphere, primarily from vehicular emissions, industrial activities and burning of fuels.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is True. Air pollution is caused by the release of harmful substances (PM, CO, SO₂, NOx and other pollutants) into the atmosphere. Major sources include vehicular exhaust, industrial emissions, power plant discharges, burning of fossil fuels and biomass, and construction activities.
elimination_hints: This is a standard definition of air pollution listing correct pollutants (PM, CO, SO₂, NOx) and correct sources (vehicles, industry, fuel burning). All elements are accurate.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Study the diagram below that shows common occupational health hazards across four different work environments — factory, office, outdoor sports and mining. Based on the diagram, which work environment shares the MOST overlapping health hazards with outdoor sports training, and what is the primary common hazard?

options.text: Office environment — because both share the common hazard of prolonged sitting and screen exposure

Mining environment — because both outdoor sports and mining share exposure to extreme heat, physical strain, dehydration risk, UV radiation and musculoskeletal injuries from repetitive physical exertion in demanding outdoor conditions
Factory environment — because both share the common hazard of exposure to toxic chemical fumes indoors
All three environments share identical hazards with outdoor sports, with no distinguishing features

explanation: Mining and outdoor sports share the most overlapping hazards: extreme heat exposure, intense physical exertion, dehydration risk, UV radiation from outdoor sun exposure, musculoskeletal injuries from repetitive physical demands, and dust/particulate inhalation. Both involve prolonged outdoor physical labour in demanding conditions, making their hazard profiles the most similar.
elimination_hints: A describes sedentary hazards (opposite of sports); C describes indoor chemical hazards; D claims no differences. B correctly identifies the outdoor physical exertion overlap between mining and sports.
subject: Physical Education
